div.p: A Maſque PRESENTED IN THE HOUSE OF THE RIGHT HONORABLE THE LORD HAYE.
BY DIVERS OF NOBLE QVALITY, HIS FRIENDS.
FOR THE ENTERTAINment of Monſieur LE BARON DE TOVR, extraordinarie Ambaſſadour for the FRENCH KING.

q.p: Quid titulum poſcis? Verſus duo tréſve legantur.

div.head: THE FRONT BEFORE THE SCENE, was an Arch-Triumphall.
div.stage: On the top of which, HVMANITIE placed in figure, ſate with her lap full of flowers, ſcattering them with her right hand; and holding a golden chaine in her left hand: to ſhew both the freedome, and the bond of Courteſie, with this inſcription.

list.head: her ſervants.
list.item: On the two ſides of the Arch

div.p:
CHEEREFVLNES, in a looſe flowing garment, filling out wine from an antique piece of plate; with this word
Adſit laetitiae dator.

READINES, a winged Mayd, with two flaming bright lights in her hands; and her word.
Amor addidit alas.
div.stage: The Scene diſcovered, is (on the one ſide) the head of a Boate, and in it CHARON putting off from the ſhore, having landed certaine imagined ghoſts, whom MERCVRY there receives, and encourageth to come on towards the River LETHE, who appeares lying in the perſon of an old man. The FATES ſitting by him on his banke; a grove of myrtles behinde them, preſented in perſpective, and growing thicker to the outer ſide of the Scene. MERCVRY, perceiving them to faint, calls them on, and ſhews them his golden rod. And the whole Maske was ſung (after the Italian manner) Stylo recitativo, by Maſter Nicholas Lanier; who ordered and made both the Scene, and the Muſicke.

lg.l: NAy, faint not now, ſo neere the fields of reſt.
Here no more furies, no more torments dwell,
Then each hath felt alreadie in his breſt;
Who hath beene once in love, hath prov'd his Hell.
Up then, and follow this my golden rod,
That points you next to aged LETHES ſhore,
Who poures his waters from his urne abroad,
Of which but taſting, you ſhall faint no more.

sp.speaker: LETHE.
sp.l: Stay, who, or what phantaſtique ſhades are theſe
That HERMES leades?

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: They are the gentle formes,
Of Lovers, toſt upon thoſe frantique ſeas,
Whence VENVS ſprung.

sp.speaker: LETHE.
sp.l: And have rid out her ſtorms?

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: No.

sp.speaker: LETHE.
sp.l: Did they periſh?

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: Yes.

sp.speaker: LETHE.
sp.l: How?

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: Drown'd by love,
That drew them forth with hopes as ſmooth as were
Th'unfaithfull waters he deſir'd them prove.

sp.speaker: LETHE.
sp.l: And turn'd a tempeſt, when he had them there?

lg.l: He did, and on the billow would he roule,
And laugh to ſee one throw his heart away,
Another ſighing, vapour forth his ſoule,
A third, to melt himſelfe in teares, and ſay,

l.hi: O Love, I now to ſalter water turne

lg.l: Then that I die in; then, a fourth, to crie
Amid the ſurges; oh! I burne, I burne:
A fift, laugh out, it is my ghoſt, not I.
And thus in paires I found'hem. Onely one
There is, that walkes, and ſtops, and ſhakes his head,
And ſhuns the reſt, as glad to be alone,
And whiſpers to himſelfe, he is not dead.

sp.speaker: FATES.
sp.l: No more are all the reſt.

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: No:

sp.speaker: 1. FATE.
sp.l: No.

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: But, why
Proceeds this doubtfull voyce from deſtiny?

sp.speaker: FATES.
sp.l: It is too ſure.

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: Sure?

sp.speaker: 2. FATE.
sp.l: I. Thinkes MERCVRY,
That any things, or names on earth doe die,
That are obſcur'd from knowledge of the FATES,
Who keepe all rolls?

sp.speaker: 3. FATE.
sp.l: And know all natures dates?

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: They ſay themſelves, th'are dead.

sp.speaker: 1. FATE.
sp.l: It not appeares.
Or, by our rocke.

sp.speaker: 2. FATE.
sp.l: Our ſpindle.

sp.speaker: 3. FATE.
sp.l: Or our ſheeres.

sp.speaker: FATES.
sp.l: Here all their threds are growing, yet none cut.

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: I'gin to doubt, that Love with charmes hath put
This phant'ſie in'hem; and they onely thinke
That they are ghoſts.

sp.speaker: FATE.
sp.l: If ſo, then let'hem drinke
Of LETHES ſtreame.

sp.speaker: FATE.
sp.l: 'Twill make'hem to forget
LOVES name.

sp.speaker: FATE.
sp.l: And ſo, they may recover yet!

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: Doe, bow unto the reverend lake:
And having touch'd there; up, and ſhake
The ſhadowes off, which yet doe make
Us you, and you your-ſelves miſtake.

stage.hi: Here they all ſtoope to the water, and dance forth their Antimaſque in ſeverall geſtures, as they liv'd in love: And retyring into the Grove, before the laſt perſon be off the Stage; the firſt couple appeare in their poſture between the trees, readie to come forth, changed.

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: See! ſee! they are themſelves agen!

sp.speaker: 1. FATE.
sp.l: Yes, now the'are ſubſtances, and men.

sp.speaker: 2. FATE.
sp.l: LOVE, at the name of LETHE flyes.

sp.speaker: LETHE.
sp.l: For, in oblivion drown'd, he dyes.

sp.speaker: 3. FATE.
sp.l: He muſt not hope, though other ſtates
He oft ſubdue, he can the FATES.

sp.speaker: FATES.
sp.l: 'Twere inſolence, to thinke his powres
Can worke on us; or equall ours.

sp.speaker: CHORVS.
sp.l: REturne, Returne,
Like lights to burne
On earth,
For others good:
Your ſecond birth
Will fame old LETHES flood,
And warne a world,
That now are hoorld
About in tempeſt, how they prove
Shadowes for Love.
Leape forth: your light it is the nobler made,
By being ſtrooke out of a ſhade.

div.stage: Here they dance forth their entrie, or firſt dance: after which

sp.speaker: CVPID—
sp.l: WHy, now you take me! theſe are rites
That grace Loves dayes, and crowne his nights!
Theſe are the motions, I would ſee,
And praiſe, in them that follow mee!
Not ſighes, nor tears, nor wounded hearts,
Nor flames, nor ghoſts: but ayrie parts
Try'd, and refin'd as yours have bin,
And ſuch they are, I glory in

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: Looke, looke unto this ſnakie rod,
And ſtop your eares, againſt the charming god;
His every word, falls from him, is a ſnare:
Who have ſo lately knowne him, ſhould beware.
sp.stage: Here they Dance their maine dance, which ended,

sp.speaker: CVPID.
sp.l: Come, doe not call it CVPIDS crime,
You were thought dead before your time.
If thus you move to HERMES will
Alone; you will be thought ſo ſtill.
Goe, take the Ladies forth, and talke,
And touch, and taſte too: Ghoſts can walke.
'Twixt eyes, tongues, hands, the mutuall ſtrife
Is bred, that tries the truth of life.
They doe, indeed, like dead men move,
That thinke they live, and not in love!
sp.stage: Here they take forth the Ladyes, and the Revells follow: after which,

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: Nay, you ſhould never have left off:
But ſtay'd, and heard your CVPID ſcoff,
To finde you in the line you were.

sp.speaker: CVPID.
sp.l: Your too much wit, breeds too much feare.

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: Good Flie, good night.

sp.speaker: CVPID.
sp.l: But will you go?
Can you leave LOVE, and he intreat you ſo?
Here, take my quiver, and my bow,
My torches too; that you, by all, may know
I meane no danger to your ſtay:
This night, I will create my holiday,
And be yours naked, and entire.

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: As if that LOVE, diſ-arm'd, were leſſe a fire?
Away, away.
sp.stage: They Dance their going out: which done,

sp.speaker: MERCVRY.
sp.l: Yet leſt that VENVS wanton Sonne,
Should with the world, be quite undone,
For your faire ſakes (you brighter ſtarres,
Who have beheld theſe civill warres.)
FATE is content, theſe Lovers here
Remaine ſtill ſuch: ſo LOVE will ſweare
Never to force them act to doo,
But what he will call HERMES too.

sp.speaker: CVPID.
sp.l: I ſweare: and with like cauſe thanke MERCVRY,
As theſe have, to thanke him, and deſtiny.

sp.speaker: CHORVS.
sp.l: All then take cauſe of joy: for who hath not?
Old LETHE, that their follies are forgot;
We, that their lives unto their fates they fit:
They, that they ſtill ſhall love, and love with wit.
